schools.summary: Matthew Henry reads the apostasy as the tragedy of prosperity - Rehoboam kept his duty while his throne tottered, then cast off religion once he felt safe; the prosperity of fools destroys them.
Gill sets down the three faithful years now ended - the tribes attached, the kingdom peaceable and prosperous while it abode by the pure worship of God.
Clarke dates the defection after the three years of 2 Chron 11:17 and frames the whole arc of the chapter to come.

Ellicott renders the opening impersonally, glosses the rare infinitive 'strengthened himself,' takes 'forsook the law' as lapsing into idolatry, and 'all Israel' as the southern kingdom counted the true Israel.

themes: forsaking the law of the Lord
strength that loosens the hold on God

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil identify Shishak as Sesonchis, first king of the 22nd dynasty, confirm the Karnak relief and its list of captured cities, and read the added clause 'because they had transgressed' as the chronicler's own philosophy of history - Shishak unconscious of the providential ground of his march.
Gill sets the sequence: apostasy in the fourth year, punishment in the fifth.
Wesley marks the invasion as following 'presently after the apostasy'; Clarke gives the Targum's 'against the Word of the Lord.'

themes: invasion as the sentence of a broken law

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott take the chariot and cavalry totals as rough estimates (Ellicott eyeing the cavalry as exactly fifty times the chariots), and gloss the Libyans, Sukkiim, and Cushites, the first and last named together as Egypt's auxiliaries in Nahum 3:9.
Clarke derives Sukkiim from a root meaning 'to cover' and makes them cave-dwelling Troglodytes (Hesychius, Pliny); Wesley reads the same name as tent-dwellers.
Gill records Josephus's reckoning of the footmen at four hundred thousand.

debates.question: What people were the Sukkiim of Shishak's army?

sides.position: cave-dwelling Troglodytes of the Egyptian Red Sea coast
a tent-dwelling people, the name meaning booths

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the fallen cities were the very bulwarks Rehoboam had raised against Egypt (2 Chron 11:5-12), still legible on the Karnak inscription (Socho, Adoraim, Ajalon), and defends the verse's historicity; Keil gives the plain sequence of capture then march on Jerusalem.
Gill: the cities Rehoboam had built and garrisoned, and in which he placed his sons, fell 'without any opposition.'

outliers.note: Cites Thenius (as reported by Ellicott) that the chronicler drew on 'really historical notices,' the Karnak reliefs corroborating the named cities.

themes: the fall of the fenced cities
stripped and thrown on forfeited mercy

schools.summary: Ellicott hears the emphatic pronoun ('Ye have forsaken me, and I also have forsaken you'), notes the Shemaiah section is peculiar to Chronicles, and reads the words as a menace of utter destruction (Neh. 9:28; Jonah 3:4); Keil reads the same measure-for-measure withdrawal.
Henry: God 'explains the rod' by the prophet - Shishak prevailed not through Judah's bad policy but because they forsook God; 'God never leaves any till they first leave him.'
Gill identifies Shemaiah as the man of God who had spoken before.
Gill reads the humbling as both inward (confessing sin and God's justice) and outward (sackcloth, fasting, prayer).
Henry hears in 'The Lord is righteous' true penitence - justifying God and judging ourselves; kings must bend or break before him.
Clarke: this humbling, absent from Kings, was 'the sole reason' Jerusalem was not destroyed and David's house not cut off.
Ellicott takes 'princes of Israel' as the princes of Judah, glosses 'humbled' as 'bowed' (2 Chron 7:14), and sets 'The Lord is righteous' beside Exod. 9:27 and Ezra 9:15.

themes: confession as the ground of mercy

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'a few for a remnant' (cf. Ezra 9:13), Keil reads the word temporally ('in a short time'); both agree 'wrath poured out' is the phrase for an exterminating judgment (2 Chron 34:25), deferred now to Nebuchadnezzar's day.
Henry hears the destroying angel forbidden to strike - 'not at this time, not by this hand'; those who own God righteous in afflicting them find him gracious.
Gill: the humiliation was 'but externally' true, yet the Lord regards even outward humbling, as he did Ahab's (1 Kings 21:29).
Wesley takes 'some deliverance' as a stop put to the course of wrath that was ready to pour out to utter destruction.

debates.question: Does the promise of 'some deliverance' mean a remnant spared or a brief respite?

sides.position: 'a few for a remnant' - a small remnant of the people preserved
'according to a little' - deliverance/respite for a short time

schools.summary: Clarke: they serve their enemies to see the difference between the service of God (perfect freedom) and of man (perfect bondage) - 'a sinful life is both expensive and painful'; Wesley: to know experimentally the two yokes.

Ellicott and Keil render 'servants for a while,' the purpose being to learn by experience the difference between the easy yoke of God and the heavy burden of foreign tyranny; 'kingdoms of the countries' from 1 Chron 29:30.
Gill takes it plainly of becoming tributaries to the king of Egypt.
Henry: they thought God's yoke too heavy; let them serve enemies in hunger and thirst (Deut. 28:47-48) - 'the service of virtue is perfect liberty; the service of lust is perfect slavery.'

Keil and Ellicott note the narrative resumes after the Shemaiah parenthesis; Shishak plundered temple and palace and bore off the golden shields of the house of the forest of Lebanon (2 Chron 9:16), word for word with 1 Kings 14:26.
Gill: Shishak settled a yearly tax on the land; 'he took all' means all he could lay hands on.
Clarke marvels at 'such a booty as never...came into the hand of man,' the golden shields being the mark of the royal guard, later imitated in Alexander's Argyraspides.
Henry: faithful David and Solomon filled the treasuries; Rehoboam, forsaking the law, emptied them - wealth held only on the tenure of obedience.

themes: wealth held on the tenure of obedience

schools.summary: Keil records that Rehoboam made brazen shields in place of the gold and gave them to the guard at the palace entrance; Ellicott ties the verse to 1 Kings 14:27 and renders 'chief of the guard' literally as 'captains of the runners.'

Ellicott recovers the frequentative ('as often as' the king went up), the runners bearing the shields and returning them to the guard-chamber (1 Kings 14:28), recalling that Solomon's gold shields hung in the house of the forest of Lebanon; Keil records the same coming and going.

Ellicott marks the remark as the chronicler's own, wanting in Kings, fulfilling the promise of v.7; 'altogether' = 'unto consumption' (Ezek. 13:13); 'good things' = a surviving faithfulness (2 Chron 19:3). Keil reads the didactic clause the same way.
Gill: Rehoboam 'broke his heart' (Targum) but was contrite only seemingly, the humbling merely external - yet God regarded it.
Henry reads 'good things' both of piety (a faithful remnant) and prosperity (affairs altering for the better).
Wesley leans to recovery: 'they began to recruit themselves, and regain some degree of their former prosperity.'

debates.question: What are the 'good things in Judah' for which the land was spared?

sides.position: a surviving faithfulness to Jehovah, a moral remnant
returning prosperity, a people beginning to recover

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil take 'strengthened himself' as a regaining of strength after Shishak's blow - seventeen years in the chosen city, the age and Ammonite mother word for word from 1 Kings 14:21.
Henry: the fenced cities having failed, Rehoboam fortified Jerusalem and reigned near the temple but far from God - honour and aggravation together; his later years less grossly apostate.
Gill: after Shishak departed Rehoboam fortified Jerusalem for his own defence.
Clarke, following Houbigant, argues 'forty-one' is an error for sixteen - Rehoboam was young, taking counsel of the young men (2 Chron 10:8) and called 'tender-hearted' (2 Chron 13:7).

debates.question: Was Rehoboam forty-one or sixteen at his accession?

sides.position: forty-one, the received reading transmitted unchallenged from Kings
sixteen; 'forty-one' is a transcriptional error, truer to a young, tender-hearted king

themes: near the temple but far from God
the disputed age of the king

schools.summary: Henry unfolds the clause into an anatomy of a divided soul: Rehoboam did not serve because he did not seek, did not pray as Solomon did, never set his heart to religion; his goodness transient as a morning cloud (Hosea 6:4).

Ellicott notes the Syriac's 'before the Lord,' the recurring phrase 'prepare the heart to seek the Lord' (2 Chron 19:3; 30:19; Ezra 7:10), and refers the estimate to the early years; Keil restates it plainly.
Gill: 'he did evil' = a relapse into idolatry, the humiliation neither hearty nor sincere.
Wesley reads 'settled not' - he humbled himself for a season but quickly relapsed, his heart not right with God.

themes: did not serve because did not seek

schools.summary: Ellicott takes 'the book' as a history and 'concerning genealogies' as 'for registration,' tracing the chronicler's peculiar matter to the records of Shemaiah and Iddo, and answers Reuss's charge of contradiction; Keil reads the 'wars' as a standing state of hostility.
Gill passes the verse quickly to the fuller account.
Clarke gives the Targum's 'concerning genealogies' as 'the book of the genealogy of the family of David.'

debates.question: Do the 'wars all the days' contradict the prophet's ban on war in 2 Chron 11:4?

sides.position: no contradiction - a permanent hostility distinct from the one forbidden campaign
the notice contradicts the earlier prohibition

themes: permanent enmity of the two kingdoms
the bitter fruit of the division

schools.summary: Keil records the death-notice plainly; Ellicott notes the verse is abridged from 1 Kings 14:31 and judges 'Abijah' (vs. Kings' 'Abijam') an accident of transcription rather than a real variant.
Clarke remarks the many varieties in the king's name, referring to his fuller note at 1 Kings 14:31.

themes: buried in the city of David
